title: R tools for epidemiology
text: yodr (phonetically: “Yoda”) small R package tools epidemiology hypothesis testing. “Size matters . Look . Judge -log10 p-value, ?” Note: v1.0.0 functionally identical lukesRlib v0.2.13 renamed something 55% shorter yet 100% better. Follows general *nix rule command >4 characters ’s worth typing. Key functions also renamed. Icon Google Gemini (Banana Nano)

title: R tools for epidemiology
text: Hypothesis testing Data Transformation Attributable Fraction Working test statistics Genetic epidemiology Plotting-related

title: R tools for epidemiology
text: install development version GitHub use remotes package:

title: R tools for epidemiology
text: tidy() runs broom::tidy() returns tidy estimates CIs calculated EST +/- 1.96*SE (.e., Wald method) Motivation: default {broom} package uses confint() estimate CIs. GLMs calculates CIs via profile likelihood method. using large datasets takes long time meaningfully alter CIs compared calculating using 1.96*SE tidy() nice things: hides intercept default, automatically detects logistic/CoxPH/CRR models exponentiates estimates, p==0 returns ‘extreme p’ string. options include -log10 p-values.

title: R tools for epidemiology
text: Provided N R^2 estimate. Calculated “extreme p” p rounded 0 Automatically identified input coxph model exponentiated estimate/CIs. Also provided N Nevents. Also, tidied “.factor()” variable names. haven::as_factor() used labels shown correctly.
code: fit_linear = glm(bmi ~ age + sex, data = d) tidy(fit_linear) #> Linear model (estimate=coefficient) :: N=449811 :: R2=0.0099 #> # A tibble: 2 x 8 #>   term  estimate std.error statistic   p.value conf.low conf.high p.extreme #>   <chr>    <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl> <chr>     #> 1 age     0.0196  0.000847     23.1  4.72e-118   0.0179    0.0212 NA        #> 2 sex     0.703   0.0137       51.4  0           0.676     0.729  9.39e-576 library(survival) fit_coxph = coxph(Surv(time, status) ~ age + sex + as.factor(smoking_status), data = d) tidy(fit_coxph) #> CoxPH model (estimate=Hazard Ratio) :: N=449811, Nevents=31025 #> # A tibble: 4 x 8 #>   term             estimate std.error statistic  p.value conf.low conf.high #>   <chr>               <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl> #> 1 age                 0.995  0.000837     -6.56 5.28e-11    0.993     0.996 #> 2 sex                 1.04   0.0109        3.66 2.52e- 4    1.02      1.06  #> 3 smoking_status-1    1.04   0.0120        3.26 1.13e- 3    1.02      1.06  #> 4 smoking_status-2    1.03   0.0149        2.16 3.08e- 2    1.00      1.06

title: R tools for epidemiology
text: phewas() (phonetically: “fee-”) makes PheWAS R easy fast. gets tidy model output categorical continuous exposures, linear, logistic, CoxPH models. Output includes N N cases, outcome, model info. User can provide multiples exposures outcomes.

what: Example: Categorical exposure in logistic regression
title: R tools for epidemiology
text: estimate Odds Ratio logistic regression model, n n_cases show numbers category exposure (af=TRUE .e., treat factor), including reference group. Note labels haven::labelled() shown correctly.
code: phewas(x="smoking_status", y="chd", z="+age", d=ukb, model="logistic", af=TRUE) #> A tibble: 3 x 12 #>   outcome exposure               estimate std.error statistic p.value conf.low conf.high     n n_cases model    #>   <chr>   <chr>                     <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>   <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl> <dbl>   <dbl> <chr>    #> 1 chd     smoking_status-Never       NA       NA         NA    NA        NA         NA    1073     146 logistic #> 2 chd     smoking_status-Former      1.24     0.126      1.72  0.0852    0.970      1.59   918     180 logistic #> 3 chd     smoking_status-Current     1.48     0.181      2.16  0.0311    1.04       2.11   285      52 logistic

what: Example: Multiple exposures on single outcome (i.e., a “PheWAS”)
title: R tools for epidemiology
text: Multiple exposures outcomes can provided simultaneously. , exposures continuous.
code: x_vars = c("bmi","ldl","sbp") phewas(x=x_vars, y="chd", z="+age+sex", d=ukb, model="logistic") #> # A tibble: 3 x 11 #>   outcome exposure estimate std.error statistic p.value conf.low conf.high     n n_cases model    #>   <chr>   <chr>       <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>   <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl> <int>   <int> <chr>    #> 1 chd     bmi         1.08    0.0113      6.97  3.1e-12    1.06       1.11  4692     324 logistic #> 2 chd     ldl         0.980   0.0689     -0.300 7.6e- 1    0.856      1.12  4498     311 logistic #> 3 chd     sbp         1.01    0.00333     3.53  4.2e- 4    1.01       1.02  4561     316 logistic

title: R tools for epidemiology
text: Data first argument. phewas() can right-side dplyr functions. Stratified models can therefore easily performed. note argument means output labelled can combined single data frame easily.
code: res_all     = ukb |>                  phewas(x=x_vars, y="chd", z="+age+sex", model="logistic", note="All") res_males   = ukb |>                  filter(sex=="Male") |>                  phewas(x=x_vars, y="chd", z="+age", model="logistic", note="Males") res_females = ukb |>                  filter(sex=="Female") |>                  phewas(x=x_vars, y="chd", z="+age", model="logistic", note="Females")  res = list_rbind(list(res_all, res_males, res_females))

title: R tools for epidemiology
text: systems OpenMP similar configurations may see good CPU usage underlying algebra/decomposiions etc can multi-threaded. systems limited reference BLAS might just see one thread used (.e., things feel slow). Fomr v1.1.0 can set parallel=TRUE phewas() use {parallely} package run parallel. Unless running many exposures/outcomes might see improvement system uses OpenMP etc. aware child process (parallel worker) uses extra RAM data copied node. standard RStudio DNAnexus instance 16Gb RAM find efficient set 2 3 child processes (everything goes faster doesn’t require big resources).

title: R tools for epidemiology
text: Inverse (quantile) normalise quantitative trait (vector) .e., transform normal distribution mean=0 sd=1
code: x_in = inv_norm(x)  df = df |> mutate(x_in = inv_norm(x))

title: R tools for epidemiology
text: Z-transform quantitative trait (vector) .e., convert mean=0 sd=1, maintaining original distribution
code: x_z = z_trans(x)  df = df |> mutate(x_z = z_trans(x))

title: R tools for epidemiology
text: Computes attributable fraction exposed (AFE) population attributable fraction (PAF) 95% Confidence Intervals (95% CIs) binary exposure (coded 0/1) either: binary outcome (prevalence / risk) y_t NULL, time--event outcome (incidence) using Cox proportional hazards model y_t provided.
code: # Binary exposure example_data$hypertension <- dplyr::if_else(example_data$sbp>=140, 1, 0)  # Binary outcome (prevalence / risk) af <- paf(   d = example_data,   x = "hypertension",   y = "event" ) cat(sprintf("PAF: %.2f%% (95%% CI: %.2f%% to %.2f%%)\n", af$paf_percent, af$paf_ci_lower_percent, af$paf_ci_upper_percent)) #> PAF: 13.75% (95% CI: 10.84% to 16.71%) cat(sprintf("Excess cases in the exposued: %1.0f (95%% CI: %1.0f to %1.0f)\n", af$excess_cases_exposed, af$excess_cases_exposed_ci_lower, af$excess_cases_exposed_ci_upper)) #> Excess cases in the exposued: 203 (95% CI: 168 to 234)

title: R tools for epidemiology
text: Calculate Standard Error Confidence Intervals.
code: lci = 0.1 uci = 0.3  # Default denominator is (1.96*2) equivalent to 95% confidence (p<0.05) get_se(lci, uci) #>  [1] 0.05102041  # custom denominator e.g., if CIs correspond to a p-value 5*10-8 get_se(lci, uci, denominator=yodr::get_z(5e-8)*2)    #>  [1] 0.01834421

title: R tools for epidemiology
text: Function estimate haplotype frequencies LD statistics. Genotype data R usually loaded genotype counts biallelic variants. Accurately estimating haplotype frequencies without phase information challenging. key ambiguity double heterozygotes (1/1): can’t tell haplotypes A1-B1/A2-B2 A1-B2/A2-B1. estimate haplotype frequency without underlying haplotype phase can use Expectation-Maximisation (EM) algorithm Excoffier & Slatkin (1995). function estimates haplotype frequencies returns R^2 D’ statistics. Estimates highly similar plink2 --r2-phased cols=+dprimeabs several examples. function combination older scripts plus input Copilot.

title: R tools for epidemiology
text: Annotate ggplot2 plot text. Allows one specify relative position figure easily. Configurable margin, text box justification. added bonus following code can specify facet annotate something like facets=data.frame(cat1='blue', cat2='tall'). Function originally Rosen Matev (https://stackoverflow.com/questions/22488563/ggplot2-annotate-layer-position--r) get aligned nicely middle, may also require adjusting box_just depending plot width etc. options include size alpha
code:
qplot(1:10,1:10) + annotate_textp('Text annotation
x=1, y=0, hjust=1', x=1, y=0, hjust=1) qplot(1:10,1:10) + annotate_textp('Text annotation
x=0.1, y=0.9, hjust=0', x=0, y=1, hjust=0) qplot(1:10,1:10) + annotate_textp('Text annotation
x = 0.5, y=0.5, hjust=0.5
box_just=c(0.5,0.5)', x=0.5, y=0.5, hjust=0.5, box_just=c(0.5,0.5)) qplot(1:10,1:10) + annotate_textp('Text annotation
x = 0.5, y=0.5, hjust=0.5
box_just=c(0.5,0.5)
size=14, alpha=0.5', x=0.5, y=0.5, hjust=0.5, box_just=c(0.5,0.5), size=13, alpha=0.5)

title: R tools for epidemiology
text: Nice modification ggplot’s theme_minimal Albert Rapp (https://alberts-newsletter.beehiiv.com/p/ggplot-theme)
code: library(tidyverse) penguins <- palmerpenguins::penguins |>    filter(!is.na(sex))  basic_plot <- penguins |>    ggplot(aes(x = bill_length_mm, y = body_mass_g, fill = species)) +   geom_point(shape = 21, size = 5, alpha = 0.85, color = 'grey10') +   labs(     x = 'Bill length (in mm)',     y = element_blank(),     fill = 'Species',     title = 'Penguins from the Palmer Archipelago',     subtitle = 'Penguin weights (in g)',     caption = 'Data: {palmerpenguins} R package'   )  basic_plot +   theme_minimal(base_size = 16, base_family = 'Source Sans Pro') +   scale_fill_manual(values = c('Adelie'= '#da2c38', 'Chinstrap'= '#FED18C', 'Gentoo'= '#30C5FF')) +  # scale_fill_manual(values = thematic::okabe_ito(6)) +   theme_minimal_modified()

what: Annotate a ggplot2 plot with text — annotate_textp
title: Annotate a ggplot2 plot with text — annotate_textp
text: One can specify relative position figure easily. Configurable margin, text box justification. added bonus following code can specify facet annotate something like facets=data.frame(cat1='blue', cat2='tall') Stack Overflow comment https://stackoverflow.com/questions/22488563/ggplot2-annotate-layer-position--r

title: Annotate a ggplot2 plot with text — annotate_textp
code: annotate_textp(   label,   x,   y,   facets = NULL,   hjust = 0,   vjust = 0,   color = "black",   alpha = NA,   family = thm$text$family,   size = thm$text$size,   fontface = 1,   lineheight = 1,   box_just = ifelse(c(x, y) < 0.5, 0, 1),   margin = grid::unit(size/2, "pt"),   thm = ggplot2::theme_get() )

title: Annotate a ggplot2 plot with text — annotate_textp
text: label string. text plotted. x Numeric. X axis position (min=0, max=1 - relative plot margins) y Numeric. Y axis position (min=0, max=1 - relative plot margins) hjust Numeric. horizontal alignment  (0 = left, 0.5 = center, 1 = right). Default=0 alpha Numeric. Transparency (0:1). Default=NA size Numeric. Font size. Default=theme default

title: Annotate a ggplot2 plot with text — annotate_textp
text: Returns geom layer

title: Annotate a ggplot2 plot with text — annotate_textp
text: Rosen Matev

title: Annotate a ggplot2 plot with text — annotate_textp
code: p1 = ggplot2::ggplot(data.frame(x=1:10,y=1:10), ggplot2::aes(x,y)) + ggplot2::geom_point() p1 + annotate_textp(label="x=1, y=0, hjust=1",                      x=1, y=0, hjust=1)  p1 + annotate_textp(label="x=0.1, y=0.9, hjust=0",                      x=0, y=0.9, hjust=0)  p1 + annotate_textp(label="x = 0.5, y=0.5, hjust=0.5, box_just=c(0.5,0.5)",                      x=0.5, y=0.5, hjust=0.5, box_just=c(0.5,0.5))  p1 + annotate_textp(label="x = 0.5, y=0.5, hjust=0.5, box_just=c(0.5,0.5), size=6, alpha=0.5",                      x=0.5, y=0.5, hjust=0.5, box_just=c(0.5,0.5), size=6, alpha=0.5)

title: Recode a Variable — carrec
text: Lifted straight Steve Miller's stevemisc package (v1.4.1). recodes numeric vector, character vector, factor according fairly simple recode specifications former Stata users appreciate. Yes, taken John Fox's recode() function car package. going carrec() (.e. shorthand car::recode(), phonetically : "car-wreck") package. goal minimize number function clashes multiple packages use workflow. example: car, dplyr, Hmisc recode() functions. rely car package just function, conflicts tidyverse functions vital workflow.

title: Recode a Variable — carrec
code: carrec(var, recodes, as_fac, as_num = TRUE, levels)

title: Recode a Variable — carrec
text: var numeric vector, character vector, factor recodes character string recode specifications: see , former Stata users find stuff familiar as_fac return factor; default TRUE var factor,  FALSE otherwise as_num TRUE (default) .factor FALSE, result coerced numeric values result numeric. want majority applications regression analysis. levels optional argument specifying order levels returned factor; default use sort order level names. ... optional, make shortcut (carr()) work

title: Recode a Variable — carrec
text: carrec() returns vector, recoded specifications user.

title: Recode a Variable — carrec
text: Recode specifications appear character string, separated semicolons (see examples ), form input=output. input value satisfies one specification, first (left right) applies. specification satisfied, input value carried result. NA allowed input output.

title: Recode a Variable — carrec
text: Fox, J. Weisberg, S. (2019). R Companion Applied Regression, Third Edition, Sage.

title: Recode a Variable — carrec
text: John Fox

what: Estimate haplotype frequencies and LD statistics from two biallelic variants — estimate_ld
title: Estimate haplotype frequencies and LD statistics from two biallelic variants — estimate_ld
text: Genotype data R usually loaded genotype counts biallelic variants. Accurately estimating haplotype frequencies without phase information challenging. key ambiguity double heterozygotes (1/1): tell haplotypes A1-B1/A2-B2 A1-B2/A2-B1. estimate haplotype frequency without underlying haplotype phase can use Expectation-Maximisation (EM) algorithm Excoffier & Slatkin (1995) (https://doi.org/10.1093/oxfordjournals.molbev.a040269). function estimates haplotype frequencies returns R^2 D' statistics. Estimates highly similar plink2 --r2-phased cols=+dprimeabs several examples. function combination older scripts plus input Copilot.

title: Estimate haplotype frequencies and LD statistics from two biallelic variants — estimate_ld
Estimate haplotype frequencies and LD statistics from two biallelic variants — estimate_ld
text: df data frame exactly two cols - must integer vector (0, 1, 2) — minor allele counts variant B tol Convergence tolerance EM max_iter Maximum EM iterations

title: Estimate haplotype frequencies and LD statistics from two biallelic variants — estimate_ld
text: data.frame haplotype freqs, allele freqs, D, D', R²

title: Estimate haplotype frequencies and LD statistics from two biallelic variants — estimate_ld
text: Luke Pilling

title: Estimate haplotype frequencies and LD statistics from two biallelic variants — estimate_ld
code: # Estimate LD between HFE p.C282Y and HFE p.S65C ukb |>    dplyr::select(rs1800562_g_a, rs1800730_a_t) |>    estimate_ld() #> Error: object 'ukb' not found #>         P_A1B1     P_A1B2     P_A2B1    P_A2B2       p_A1      p_A2       p_B1     p_B2            D       D_max    D_prime unsigned_D_prime   R_squared em_iterations #> 1 3.203526e-05 0.07335235 0.01563897 0.9109766 0.07338439 0.9266156 0.01567101 0.984329 -0.001117972 0.001150007 -0.9721434        0.9721434 0.001191575           110

what: Estimate attributable fraction in the exposed (AFE) and population attributable fraction (PAF) — paf
title: Estimate attributable fraction in the exposed (AFE) and population attributable fraction (PAF) — paf
text: Computes attributable fraction exposed (AFE) population attributable fraction (PAF) 95% Confidence Intervals (95% CIs) binary exposure (coded 0/1) either: binary outcome (prevalence / risk) y_t NULL, time--event outcome (incidence) using Cox proportional hazards model y_t provided. Binary-outcome case: logistic regression fitted AFE/PAF estimated standardisation (g-formula): mean predicted risks observed exposure counterfactual exposure assignments z != "" (.e., covariates provided) logistic regression adjusted. n_boot > 0, non-parametric bootstrap 95% CIs returned AFE/PAF. Time--event case: Cox model fitted hazard ratio (HR) exposure used effect measure. Wald 95% confidence intervals taken Cox model, transformed AFE PAF. Crude incidence rates (per 1,000 person-years) also returned exposure group (assumes time years).

title: Estimate attributable fraction in the exposed (AFE) and population attributable fraction (PAF) — paf
code: paf(   d,   x,   y,   y_t = NULL,   z = "",   n_boot = 0L,   skip_boot = TRUE,   use_parallel = FALSE,   n_child = floor(parallelly::availableCores()/3),   verbose = FALSE )

title: Estimate attributable fraction in the exposed (AFE) and population attributable fraction (PAF) — paf
text: d data frame containing exposure, outcome, (optionally) follow-time covariates. x Character string. Column name exposure variable (expected 0/1). y Character string. Column name outcome variable (0/1; event indicator survival). y_t Character string. Optional column name follow-time time--event analyses. Ideally years, crude incidence rates (per 1,000 person-years) also returned. provided treats y binary outcome estimates AFE/PAF. default=NULL (character) z string. Covariate formula additions (e.g., "+age+sex") variables found d. y_t provided, covariates included Cox model. y_t NULL z != "", covariates included logistic regression AFE/PAF estimated standardisation (g-formula). default="" (character) n_boot Integer. Number bootstrap replicates adjusted (logistic) binary-outcome AFE/PAF. used y_t NULL z != "". 0 bootstrap CIs computed. default=0 (integer) skip_boot Logical. regression significant skip bootstrapping PAF CIs default=TRUE use_parallel Logical. Use parallel processing bootstraps? default=FALSE n_child Numeric. Number child processes create parallel processing. Default fraction total cores available avoid crashing cloud instances due RAM limits. default=(total cores available)/3 verbose Logical. verbose, default=FALSE.

title: Estimate attributable fraction in the exposed (AFE) and population attributable fraction (PAF) — paf
text: data frame containing AFE PAF (proportions percentages) 95% CIs p-values, plus supporting statistics (counts, ORs, excess cases exposed, etc.).

title: Estimate attributable fraction in the exposed (AFE) and population attributable fraction (PAF) — paf
text: Coding assumptions: x coded 0/1 (unexposed/exposed). binary-outcome case, y 0/1. time--event case, y event indicator (0/1) y_t follow-time (years want rates per 1,000 person-years).

title: Estimate attributable fraction in the exposed (AFE) and population attributable fraction (PAF) — paf
text: Luke Pilling

title: Estimate attributable fraction in the exposed (AFE) and population attributable fraction (PAF) — paf
code: # Binary exposure example_data$hypertension <- dplyr::if_else(example_data$sbp>=140, 1, 0)    # Binary outcome (prevalence / risk) crude res1 <- paf(   d = example_data,   x = "hypertension",   y = "event" ) #> ℹ yodr v1.1.1  # Binary outcome adjusted res1_adj <- paf(   d = example_data,   x = "hypertension",   y = "event",   z = "age+sex",   n_boot = 0 ) #> ℹ yodr v1.1.1  # Binary outcome adjusted res1_adj_cis <- paf(   d = example_data,   x = "hypertension",   y = "event",   z = "age+sex",   n_boot = 100 ) #> ℹ yodr v1.1.1 #> → Bootstrapping adjusted AFE/PAF CIs with 100 iterations (can take a while)  #rbind(res1, res1_adj, res1_adj_cis)  # Time-to-event outcome (incidence) res2 <- paf(   d = example_data,   x = "hypertension",   y = "event",   y_t = "time",   z = "age+sex" ) #> ℹ yodr v1.1.1

what: PheWAS of exposure(s) on outcome(s) with tidy model output — phewas
title: PheWAS of exposure(s) on outcome(s) with tidy model output — phewas
text: easily get tidy model output (linear logistic GLM, CoxPH) categorical continuous exposure, including sample size (N cases logistic), outcome, model info. Idea make quick PheWAS trivially easy. exposures, gets N. categorical exposures, N split group, row included reference category Can provide multiple exposures /outcomes Options scale inverse normal transform exposure /outcome, winsorize exposure /outcome (values beyond N SDs mean set N). Options return model fit statistic (R^2 linear, pseudo-R^2 logistic, C-statistic CoxPH) include regression formula output. Options return estimates independent variables model (just exposure interest) include interaction terms specified variable.

title: PheWAS of exposure(s) on outcome(s) with tidy model output — phewas
code: phewas(   d,   x,   y,   z = "",   model = "lm",   af = FALSE,   af_base = FALSE,   note = "",   get_fit = FALSE,   extreme_ps = FALSE,   include_formula = FALSE,   scale_x = FALSE,   scale_y = FALSE,   inv_norm_x = FALSE,   inv_norm_y = FALSE,   winsorize_x = FALSE,   winsorize_y = FALSE,   winsorize_n = 5,   return_all_terms = FALSE,   interacts_with = "",   parallel = FALSE,   n_child = floor(parallelly::availableCores()/3),   progress = TRUE,   verbose = FALSE,   ... )  get_assoc(x, ...)

title: PheWAS of exposure(s) on outcome(s) with tidy model output — phewas
text: d data.frame tibble. data. x string vector strings. exposure variable name(s), found d. (character) y string. outcome variable name, found d – model 'coxph' paste survival object e.g., 'Surv(time, event)' time event variables d. (character) z string. covariate formula (e.g., " + age + sex"), found d. Default covariates. default="" (character) model string. type model perform. Can "lm", "logistic" "coxph". default="lm" (character) af Logical. x categorical? .e., include formula like haven::as_factor(x). use base R .factor() instead set option "af_base" TRUE default=FALSE af_base Logical. Use base R .factor() instead haven::as_factor(x)? default=FALSE note string. want include note like "", "Males", "C282Y homozygotes" describe model sample. default="" (character) get_fit Logical. Default FALSE. Get model fit? (model: lm=R2, logistic=McFadden's pseudo-R2, coxph=Harrell's c-statistic). default=FALSE extreme_ps Logical. Default FALSE. p==0 return "extreme p-values" strings. default=FALSE include_formula Logical. Default FALSE. Include regression formula output? default=FALSE scale_x Logical. Default FALSE. Apply scale() function exposure? default=FALSE scale_y Logical. Default FALSE. Apply scale() function outcome? default=FALSE inv_norm_x Logical. Apply inv_norm() function exposure? default=FALSE inv_norm_y Logical. Apply inv_norm() function outcome? default=FALSE winsorize_x Logical. Apply Winzorization exposure? default=FALSE winsorize_y Logical. Apply Winzorization outcome? default=FALSE winsorize_n Numeric. Standard deviations mean Winzorize. .e., participants values beyond N set N. default=5 return_all_terms Logical. Return estimates independent variables (terms) model? TRUE, includes adddition column 'term' default=FALSE interacts_with string. variable found d. add regression formula like x*catch output default="" (character) parallel Logical. Run parallel using parallel package? FALSE, run sequentially. Parallel processing can speed analysis many exposures /outcomes, aware uses RAM can slower small numbers exposures/outcomes due overhead parallelization. default=FALSE n_child Numeric. Number child processes create parallel processing. Default fraction total cores available avoid crashing cloud instances due RAM limits. default=(total cores available)/3 progress Logical. Show progress bar purrr map() function (useful multiple exposures/outcomes provided). default=TRUE verbose Logical. verbose, default=FALSE ... options passed internally

title: PheWAS of exposure(s) on outcome(s) with tidy model output — phewas
text: Returns tibble - summary statistics model

title: PheWAS of exposure(s) on outcome(s) with tidy model output — phewas
text: get_assoc(): Alias phewas

title: PheWAS of exposure(s) on outcome(s) with tidy model output — phewas
text: Luke Pilling

title: PheWAS of exposure(s) on outcome(s) with tidy model output — phewas
code: # for one outcome, equivalent to `tidy_ci(glm(sbp ~ bmi +age+sex, d=example_data))` - with added `n` phewas(x="bmi", y="sbp", z="+age+sex", d=example_data) #> ℹ yodr v1.1.1 #> → Getting 1 association #> ✔ Finished. Time taken: 154ms. #> # A tibble: 1 × 10 #>   outcome exposure estimate std.error statistic  p.value conf.low conf.high #>   <chr>   <chr>       <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl> #> 1 sbp     bmi          1.49    0.0730      20.4 5.42e-89     1.35      1.64 #> # ℹ 2 more variables: n <int>, model <chr>  # categorical exposure, binary outcome, and stratified analysis (with note) #  - note that data can be passed using the pipe if desired example_data |> dplyr::filter(sex==1) |>   phewas(x="bmi_cat", y="event", z="+age", model="logistic", af=TRUE, note="Males only")  |> print(width=500) #> ℹ yodr v1.1.1 #> → Getting 1 association #> ✔ Finished. Time taken: 70ms. #> # A tibble: 3 × 12 #>   outcome exposure           estimate std.error statistic    p.value conf.low #>   <chr>   <chr>                 <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>      <dbl>    <dbl> #> 1 event   bmi_cat-Normal        NA      NA          NA    NA            NA    #> 2 event   bmi_cat-Overweight     1.50    0.0986      4.09  0.0000426     1.23 #> 3 event   bmi_cat-Obese          1.93    0.154       4.27  0.0000194     1.43 #>   conf.high     n n_cases model    note       #>       <dbl> <dbl>   <dbl> <chr>    <chr>      #> 1     NA      803     268 logistic Males only #> 2      1.82  1018     443 logistic Males only #> 3      2.60   226     111 logistic Males only  # multiple exposures and/or outcomes - get pseudo R^2 x_vars = c("bmi","sbp","dbp","scl") y_vars = c("event","sex") phewas(x=x_vars, y=y_vars, z="+age", d=example_data, model="logistic", get_fit=TRUE)  |> print(width=500) #> ℹ yodr v1.1.1 #> → Getting 8 associations #> ✔ Finished. Time taken: 602ms. #> # A tibble: 8 × 13 #>   outcome exposure estimate std.error statistic  p.value conf.low conf.high #>   <chr>   <chr>       <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl> #> 1 event   bmi         1.07   0.00791       9.07 1.19e-19    1.06       1.09 #> 2 sex     bmi         1.04   0.00736       5.09 3.54e- 7    1.02       1.05 #> 3 event   sbp         1.01   0.00149       8.86 7.80e-19    1.01       1.02 #> 4 sex     sbp         0.998  0.00141      -1.12 2.61e- 1    0.996      1.00 #> 5 event   dbp         1.03   0.00259       9.73 2.33e-22    1.02       1.03 #> 6 sex     dbp         1.01   0.00240       4.98 6.35e- 7    1.01       1.02 #> 7 event   scl         1.01   0.000749      9.47 2.69e-21    1.01       1.01 #> 8 sex     scl         0.999  0.000692     -1.08 2.82e- 1    0.998      1.00 #>       n n_cases fit_stat fit_stat_se model    #>   <int>   <int>    <dbl> <lgl>       <chr>    #> 1  4690    1472 0.0317   NA          logistic #> 2  4690    2047 0.00454  NA          logistic #> 3  4699    1473 0.0310   NA          logistic #> 4  4699    2049 0.000666 NA          logistic #> 5  4699    1473 0.0338   NA          logistic #> 6  4699    2049 0.00435  NA          logistic #> 7  4666    1466 0.0331   NA          logistic #> 8  4666    2042 0.000649 NA          logistic  # if desired, can also return estimates for other independent variables (terms) in the model x_vars = c("bmi","sbp","dbp") phewas(x=x_vars, y="event", z="+age+sex", d=example_data, model="logistic", return_all_terms=TRUE)  |> print(width=500) #> ℹ yodr v1.1.1 #> → Getting 3 associations #> ✔ Finished. Time taken: 162ms. #> # A tibble: 9 × 12 #>   outcome exposure term  estimate std.error statistic  p.value conf.low #>   <chr>   <chr>    <chr>    <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl> #> 1 event   bmi      bmi       1.07   0.00801      8.66 4.74e-18     1.06 #> 2 event   bmi      age       1.04   0.00388      8.95 3.47e-19     1.03 #> 3 event   bmi      sex       2.10   0.0654      11.3  1.02e-29     1.84 #> 4 event   sbp      sbp       1.01   0.00152      9.47 2.86e-21     1.01 #> 5 event   sbp      age       1.03   0.00412      6.15 7.89e-10     1.02 #> 6 event   sbp      sex       2.23   0.0658      12.2  4.15e-34     1.96 #> 7 event   dbp      dbp       1.02   0.00261      9.24 2.43e-20     1.02 #> 8 event   dbp      age       1.03   0.00394      8.07 7.03e-16     1.02 #> 9 event   dbp      sex       2.10   0.0655      11.3  1.01e-29     1.85 #>   conf.high     n n_cases model    #>       <dbl> <int>   <int> <chr>    #> 1      1.09  4690    1472 logistic #> 2      1.04    NA      NA logistic #> 3      2.38    NA      NA logistic #> 4      1.02  4699    1473 logistic #> 5      1.03    NA      NA logistic #> 6      2.53    NA      NA logistic #> 7      1.03  4699    1473 logistic #> 8      1.04    NA      NA logistic #> 9      2.39    NA      NA logistic

title: Modified theme_minimal for ggplots — theme_minimal_modified
code: penguins = palmerpenguins::penguins |>    dplyr::filter(!is.na(sex))  basic_plot = penguins |>    ggplot2::ggplot(ggplot2::aes(x = bill_length_mm, y = body_mass_g, fill = species)) +   ggplot2::geom_point(shape = 21, size = 5, alpha = 0.85, color = 'grey10') +   ggplot2::labs(     x = 'Bill length (in mm)',     y = ggplot2::element_blank(),     fill = 'Species',     title = 'Penguins from the Palmer Archipelago',     subtitle = 'Penguin weights (in g)',     caption = 'Data: {palmerpenguins} R package'   )  basic_plot +   ggplot2::theme_minimal(base_size = 16, base_family = 'Source Sans Pro') +   ggplot2::scale_fill_manual(values = c('Adelie'= '#da2c38', 'Chinstrap'= '#FED18C', 'Gentoo'= '#30C5FF')) +   theme_minimal_modified() #> Error in theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank(), panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "grey90",     linetype = 2), plot.title.position = "plot", plot.caption.position = "plot",     plot.title = element_text(family = "Cambria", size = rel(1.7),         margin = margin(b = 7, unit = "mm")), plot.subtitle = element_text(size = rel(1.1)),     text = element_text(color = "grey20"), axis.text = element_text(color = "grey30"),     panel.background = element_rect(color = "grey90")): could not find function "theme"

what: Get tidy model output with fast CIs — tidy
title: Get tidy model output with fast CIs — tidy
text: default (amazing) broom package uses confint() function calculate CIs. GLMs calculates confidence intervals via profile likelihood default. using large datasets takes long time meaningfully alter CIs compared simply calculating using 1.96SE. function tidy() runs broom::tidy() returns tidy estimates CIs calculated EST +/- 1.96SE. (Well, actually 1.959964) function also nice/useful things output: hides intercept default, automatically detects logistic/CoxPH/CRR models exponentiates estimates, p=0 returns extreme p string. optional outputs include -log10 p-values. tested models glm() survival::coxph() seems work well produces consistent CIs. Also works well cmprsk::crr(). linear mixed-effects models lmer() package lme4 can display fixed-effects p-values Wald CIs.

title: Get tidy model output with fast CIs — tidy
code: tidy(   x,   ci = TRUE,   ci_denominator = 1.959964,   exp = FALSE,   intercept = FALSE,   tidy_factors = TRUE,   extreme_ps = TRUE,   neglog10p = FALSE,   check_model = TRUE,   n = NA,   get_r2 = TRUE,   conf.int = FALSE,   quiet = FALSE,   ... )  tidy_ci(x, ...)

title: Get tidy model output with fast CIs — tidy
text: x object containing model output tidied e.g., glm() survival::coxph(). ci Logical. Default TRUE. Calculate CIs using 1.96*SE method - 1.96 can modified using ci_denominator. ci_denominator Numeric. Default 1.96  (actually 1.959964). standard error sample mean. exp Logical. Default FALSE. Exponentiate estimate CIs – also see check_model. intercept Logical. Default FALSE. Exclude intercept tidier output. tidy_factors Logical. Default TRUE. Tidy .factor(x_var)# terms x_var-#. extreme_ps Logical. Default TRUE. p=0 return "extreme p-values" strings. neglog10p Logical. Default FALSE. Provides negative log10 p-values (input class glm coxph crr – user can provide sample size n=# override). check_model Logical. Default TRUE. Set exp=TRUE glm(family=binomial) survival::coxph() cmprsk::crr() performed. n Numeric. Default NA. N neglog10p extracted automatically glm coxph objects - override required. get_r2 Logical. Default TRUE. get R^2 value model linear, C-statistic model CoxPH quiet Logical. Default FALSE. Suppress text output ... tidy() options

title: Get tidy model output with fast CIs — tidy
text: Returns tibble - summary statistics model

title: Get tidy model output with fast CIs — tidy
text: tidy_ci(): Alias tidy

title: Get tidy model output with fast CIs — tidy
text: Luke Pilling

title: Get tidy model output with fast CIs — tidy
code: fit_linear = glm(sbp ~ age + sex + as.factor(bmi_cat), data = example_data) tidy(fit_linear) #> Linear model (estimate=coefficient) :: N=4690 :: R2=0.21 #> # A tibble: 4 × 7 #>   term      estimate std.error statistic   p.value conf.low conf.high #>   <chr>        <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl> #> 1 age          0.951    0.0353     26.9  1.45e-148    0.882     1.02  #> 2 sex         -1.48     0.606      -2.44 1.48e-  2   -2.67     -0.291 #> 3 bmi_cat-1    7.40     0.652      11.4  1.70e- 29    6.13      8.68  #> 4 bmi_cat-2   16.3      0.940      17.3  2.78e- 65   14.4      18.1    fit_logistic = glm(event ~ age + sex + bmi, data = example_data, family = binomial) tidy(fit_logistic)   # detect model and exponentiate automatically #> Binomial model (estimate=Odds Ratio) :: N=4690, Ncases=1472 #> # A tibble: 3 × 7 #>   term  estimate std.error statistic  p.value conf.low conf.high #>   <chr>    <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl> #> 1 age       1.04   0.00388      8.95 3.47e-19     1.03      1.04 #> 2 sex       2.10   0.0654      11.3  1.02e-29     1.84      2.38 #> 3 bmi       1.07   0.00801      8.66 4.74e-18     1.06      1.09

title: Z-transform a variable — z_trans
text: Z-transform variable. Mean=0, SD=1. Maintains original distribution.

title: Z-transform a variable — z_trans
text: x numeric vector z-transformed

title: Z-transform a variable — z_trans
text: Returns vector

title: Z-transform a variable — z_trans
text: Luke Pilling

title: Z-transform a variable — z_trans
code: summary(example_data$sbp) #>    Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max.  #>    80.0   116.0   130.0   132.8   144.0   270.0  sbp_in = z_trans(example_data$sbp) summary(sbp_in) #>    Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max.  #> -2.3142 -0.7353 -0.1213  0.0000  0.4927  6.0187

title: yodr 1.1.1 (26th March 2026)
text: estimate AFE/PAF adjusted logistic regression models. CIs estimated non-parametric bootstrap. provides estimates excess cases exposed (95% CIs) improvements estimates output generally parallel option help speed bootstrapping

title: yodr 1.1.0 (17th March 2026)
text: phewas can now utilise cores running parallel=TRUE {parallel} package added dependencies New function estimate_ld estimate haplotype frequencies LD statistics two biallelic variants Can simple binary outcome (prevalence / risk) time--event outcome (incidence) uses Cox proportional hazards model

title: yodr 1.0.0 (22nd Feb 2026)
text: Package renamed something 55% shorter 100% better. Follows general *nix rule command >4 characters ’s worth typing. Also, yoda. Renamed tidy_ci tidy get_assoc est spirit (couldn’t work make Star Wars themed whilst also max 4 characters) Together, means kept type lukesRlib::tidy_ci(fit) now can type yodr::tidy(fit) happer Original package lcpilling/lukesRlib still unchanged old scripts still work
